topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery notification blinked on the screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into my afternoon. A package. Addressed to the previous occupant. I snatched it from the porch, the cardboard rough beneath my fingertips, and immediately felt a tight knot constrict in my chest. It was a box filled with what looked like old, dusty books and a brittle, yellowed photograph. I stared at the return address, the handwriting a familiar scrawl from a life I’d long since erased. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. I didn't know who had the audacity to leave this behind, or worse, to think I wouldn't notice. I slammed the door shut, the sound echoing in the sudden silence of my apartment, and paced the length of the living room, my breathing shallow and quick. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to snap.

[story 2]

I found the package tucked haphazardly behind the recycling bins, a glaring piece of evidence of someone else’s carelessness. When I opened it, the contents—a collection of faded postcards and a handwritten journal—sent a wave of intense irritation washing over me. My hands shook slightly as I picked up the items, the paper crinkling under the pressure. *How dare they?* The thought hammered relentlessly in my mind. I dropped the box onto the floor with a sharp thud. I walked to the window, gripping the sill until my knuckles were white, watching the street below as if expecting the responsible party to materialize and apologize. I wanted to scream, to demand an explanation, but the sound caught in my throat, leaving only a hot, prickly sensation behind my eyes.

[story 3]

It was sitting on my doorstep, a silent accusation. A package clearly meant for the tenant who had vacated weeks ago. I didn't bother with pleasantries; I just hauled it inside, the weight of the cardboard feeling disproportionately heavy. My gaze swept over the contents—a collection of strange, unlabeled vials and a heavily annotated map. A low, guttural sound escaped my lips, a sound more like a growl than a human utterance. I threw the box onto the kitchen counter, the impact jarring. I began pacing back and forth in a tight circle, my shoulders hunched, my entire posture radiating a sharp, contained hostility. I needed to know who this was, and why they had chosen this method of communication.
